name: ClearFake Campaign Attack Flow
description: Attack flow describing the ClearFake campaign based on observed behavior from compromised websites through user-assisted execution and downstream payload activity.

objects.name: Drive-by Compromise
objects.description: The victim visits a legitimate but compromised website hosting malicious ClearFake JavaScript.

objects.name: Malicious JavaScript Execution
objects.description: Malicious JavaScript executes in the victim's browser to profile the environment and redirect the user to attacker-controlled content.

objects.name: Smart Contract-Based Stage Retrieval
objects.description: The injected JavaScript interacts with a Binance Smart Chain (BSC) smart contract, leveraging the blockchain as a web-based service (MITRE ATT&CK T1102) to retrieve staged configuration data. The smart contract functions as a decentralized staging mechanism that provides next-stage instructions, which may include URLs or command sequences used in subsequent payload delivery. This design enables attackers to dynamically update delivery logic without modifying the compromised website itself.

objects.name: User-Assisted Execution Preparation
objects.description: The victim is redirected to an attacker-controlled page presenting a fake browser update or verification prompt.

objects.name: User-Assisted Execution
objects.description: The victim manually copies and executes attacker-provided commands, transitioning the attack from browser-based activity to endpoint execution.

objects.name: Command Execution via Windows Command Shell
objects.description: The user-executed command invokes cmd.exe to initiate follow-on malicious activity.

objects.name: Encoded PowerShell Execution
objects.description: PowerShell is executed with encoded commands to download and execute additional payloads.

objects.name: Payload Download
objects.description: Following user-assisted execution, the ClearFake framework retrieves and delivers secondary malware families from attacker-controlled or third-party infrastructure. Observed payloads include Amadey, SocGholish, RaccoonStealer, StealC, HijackLoader, SystemBC, and LummaStealer. Subsequent credential theft, persistence, and command-and-control activity are performed by the delivered malware rather than the ClearFake framework itself.

objects.name: Command Obfuscation
objects.description: Commands and scripts are obfuscated to evade detection and hinder analysis.

objects.name: Masquerading
objects.description: Malicious payloads and execution artifacts masquerade as legitimate software components.

objects.name: Sandbox / Virtualization Evasion
objects.description: The payload performs system and virtualization checks to evade sandbox or automated analysis environments.

objects.name: WMI-Based Environment Checks
objects.description: Windows Management Instrumentation queries are used as part of system and environment checks prior to further execution.

objects.name: Local Groups Discovery
objects.description: The malware enumerates local user groups and system accounts.

objects.name: Browser Credential Collection
objects.description: Downstream payload accesses browser credential stores to extract saved credentials and session data.

objects.name: Command and Control over Web Protocols
objects.description: The compromised host communicates with command-and-control infrastructure over HTTP or HTTPS.

objects.name: Persistence via Scheduled Task
objects.description: Persistence is established through the creation of scheduled tasks.
